page-title: How I Use OpenClaw and AI Skills to Run My Projects
s0-subtitle: PRINCE2, PMBoK or PMI for dummies like me
s0-org: Founder, ALT-F1 SRL · Partner, XFlowData · Brussels & Tunisia
s1-body:
Project management tools are powerful. OpenProject, Jira, HubSpot — they can do almost anything. But "can do" and "actually get things done" are separated by a simple gap: the manual effort.

Setting up a project properly — creating work streams, work packages, backlogs, sprints, assigning members, configuring versions, linking dependencies, writing descriptions, organizing categories — takes hours.

Not because it's hard. Because it's tedious. And tedious work gets skipped or postponed.

The result? Projects start with a Gantt chart that looks great on Monday and is outdated by Wednesday.

I tried, by chance to fix that.
s2-body:
I got back to one of the many projects I never finished because that was too complex to build at the time.

Here is the name of the directory ... it says it all "2017-10 Project management by"

The description of the project resided in one screenshot named "smart editor.PNG".

The idea was simple: write in plain English a description of the activity and produces the equivalent in a structured database schema like Jira, Wrike etc: who does what, when, for how long, list dependencies etc etc
s3-phase-1-desc: I tried a first time implementing Natural Language Processing (NLP) using NLTK. Unfortunately NLTK required mastering advanced linguistic theories, probabilistic models, and sophisticated text-processing techniques, which made it particularly challenging at the time for me.
s3-phase-2-desc: I tried a second time in 2019 using Rasa, an Open source machine learning framework; It was the beginning of the usage of the GPUs to mine Bitcoins
s3-phase-3-desc: Then a third time somewhere in 2020 Microsoft's Language Understanding Intelligent Service LUIS, a cloud-based NLP tool but it didn't work neither.
s3-phase-4-desc: In February 2026, I decided to give it a try using OpenClaw combined with Large Language Models (LLM) and Skills I implemented. It took me a dozen of hours to reach my initial goal :-/
s4-body-1: I have learned about OpenClaw in February ... after reading the documentation I decided to pass this technology — I thought it was again a useless agent.
s4-body-2:
My colleague, Jef Minsart from XFlowdata told me that he was participating to a meetup.com event organized by Toon Vanagt OpenClaw User Group Belgium.

Jef "forced" me indirectly to study the technology.
s4-body-3:
In one sentence, OpenClaw is a Personal AI Assistant (PAT) running like a Linux Server : 24/7.

The user interacts with AI Agents through any Messaging platform like WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Signal, Slack, Mattermost.
s4-body-4:
The difference with Chat Bots like ChatGPT, Gemini or Claude is that the server is alive and can interact with other systems even if you are not "connected".

For example, every morning I receive "the list of tasks updated in last 72h" in my feed.
s4-body-5:
Nothing crazy one may say ?!?

Except that I asked to my "OpenProject or Jira Skill" to generate it using a simple sentence.
s4-body-6:
A skill is a packaged capability, a set of tools, scripts, and instructions that let the agent interact with real systems like Jira, HubSpot, SAP, Salesforce, Microsoft SharePoint, ServiceNow, Check Point Management, Fortinet, Google Cloud Dataplex, Kaseya etc ...

I built one of those skills. And it changed how I manage projects.
s5-body: The openproject-by-altf1be skill gives OpenClaw full operational access to an OpenProject instance via the API v3. OpenProject is a powerful Opensource Program/Project management system. We use it for our internal projects at XFlowdata.
s5-col-core-1: Work packages — create, read, update, delete, filter
s5-col-core-2: Projects — create, configure, manage members and roles
s5-col-core-3: Members & roles — add users, assign roles
s5-col-core-4: Versions/milestones — create sprints, set date ranges
s5-col-ext-1: Comments & attachments — add context to any work package
s5-col-ext-3: Notifications, time entries, watchers, wiki pages
s5-body-2: All of this is accessible through natural language in a Discord channel.
s5-caption: Request made to OpenProject through an OpenClaw skill
s6-body: This week, I needed to set up a new project: Mistral OCR Mastering — a 10-day sprint to explore Mistral AI's document processing capabilities and build a proof-of-concept.
s6-phase-1-desc: "create a project mistral-ocr-mastering" → OpenClaw created the project on OpenProject in seconds.
s6-phase-2-desc: "add Clémentine, Mohamed, Jeanne, Jean, Pauline as members. add Béatrice, Pierre and Oumaima as managers" → Five memberships + three with Project Admin roles. No clicking through settings pages.
s6-phase-3-desc: Dropped a .docx file into the chat with meeting notes and said "write an overview of the project" → OpenClaw extracted the content, synthesized it into a structured project description, and pushed it directly to OpenProject. In markdown. With links.
s6-phase-4-desc: Shared a photo of our architecture whiteboard and said "create work packages categories" → OpenClaw analyzed the image, identified workflow areas, and created 7 fully described work packages — each with acceptance criteria, technical details, and the right type.
s7-phase-5-desc: "create versions" → 5 versions created. "the project should be finished in 10 network days" → All recompressed into 2-day sprints: March 30 → April 10.
s7-phase-6-desc: "assign tasks to Jeanne, assign accountable Oumaima, set the category itself, set the version itself" → All 7 work packages updated in one command.
s7-phase-7-desc: "add missing tasks to deploy the application on our staging environment" → 7 deployment work packages created with sequential dependencies, detailed steps, and acceptance criteria.
s7-phase-8-desc: "add a news" → A full project kickoff announcement published to the OpenProject news feed — team, objectives, timeline, links — all generated from context.
s7-body: Total time: about 45 minutes of conversation. What would this normally take? Conservatively, 4–5 hours of clicking, typing, correcting, browsing and context-switching between browser tabs, Excel, Word, Draw.io, Shared drives, Emails...
s8-phase-1-desc: The #1 killer of project management quality is incomplete setup. When creating a work package takes 2 minutes of clicking, people create vague one-liners with no description, no assignee, no version, no dependencies. When creating a work package takes 0 effort — because you describe it in natural language and the agent handles the rest — every work package gets a proper description, acceptance criteria, and metadata from day one.
s8-phase-2-desc: I manage multiple projects across OpenProject, Jira, HubSpot, and GitHub. The OpenClaw skill approach means I interact with all of them the same way: through conversation. The agent handles the API differences. Need to mirror a user's memberships across 14 projects? "make Béatrice member of the same projects as Jeanne" — Done. Seven new memberships created, all with matching roles. In one sentence.
s8-phase-3-desc: Every work package I create through OpenClaw and my Skills gets: A structured description, Steps and acceptance criteria, Links to relevant resources (SharePoint, GitLab, docs), Proper categorization and versioning. Not because I'm disciplined. Because the agent does it by default. I master the theory and learned from the best. Now the machine enforces what I always meant to do.
s8-phase-4-desc: My #bot-openproject Discord channel is now an operations console. I can: Create and manage projects, Query work package status, Generate reports and export them as files, Add members, set roles, configure versions — All without opening a browser. This matters for speed, but it matters even more for continuity. Every command and its result is visible in the chat history. It's a natural audit trail.
s9-body: OpenProject is one side of my project management stack. But I also use Atlassian Jira. So I built a second skill: atlassian-jira-by-altf1be. Same architecture. Same design principles. Different API.
s9-terminal-comment: # No browser. No board. No drag-and-drop.
s9-body-2:
What It Covers
The Jira skill gives OpenClaw full CRUD access to Jira Cloud via the REST API v3:
• Issues — create, read, update, delete, list, and search with JQL
• Comments — add, update, delete, list on any issue
• Attachments — upload, list, delete
• Workflow transitions — list available transitions and move issues through states

That last one matters more than it sounds. Workflow transitions in Jira are where tickets go to die — stuck in "In Review" because nobody clicked the button.
s9-body-3:
The Same Patterns Work
Everything I described for OpenProject applies to Jira:
• Creating issues with full descriptions, priorities, and assignees — from a sentence
• Bulk operations across projects
• Attaching files from the chat
• Moving issues through workflows without opening a browser
• Commenting on issues as part of a conversation
• Add those work items into a new sprint and start the sprint !

The mental model is identical: describe what you want, the agent handles the API.
s9-body-4:
Security: Same Standards
Both skills share the same security posture:
• Auth — email + API token (Basic auth, base64 encoded)
• No secrets to stdout — tokens never appear in logs or output
• Destructive operations require --confirm — no accidental deletes
• Rate limiting with exponential backoff — respects Atlassian's API limits
• Lazy config validation — credentials are only checked when you actually run a command
• Path traversal prevention — file uploads are sandboxed
s9-body-5:
Why Two Skills Instead of One? OpenProject and Jira are fundamentally different systems. Different APIs, different data models, different workflow engines. The agent layer (OpenClaw) provides the unified interface.

#bot-openproject → OpenProject skill
#bot-jira → Jira skill

I create Discord threads to keep the context of the projects separated:
#bot-openproject → mistral-ocr-mastering
#bot-jira → atlassian-jira-ui

Same human. Same language. Different backends.
s10-body: The OpenProject skill is one of many. OpenClaw's ClawHub hosts skills for:
s10-step-1-desc: Full CRUD, workflow transitions, JQL search
s10-body-2:
Each skill follows the same pattern: a Command Line Interpreter (CLI) tool that the agent knows how to use, backed by API calls with proper auth, rate limiting, and error handling.

You can also build your own skills. The OpenProject skill I use started as a Proof of Concept (PoC) — a Node.js CLI with Commander, dotenv, and the OpenProject API v3. OpenClaw's skill spec makes it straightforward to package and share.
s11-phase-1-title: AI never ever replace project managers. It removes the friction.
s11-phase-1-desc: The decisions are still mine: what to build, who to assign, how to prioritize. But the execution of those decisions — the clicking, the typing, the formatting, the linking — that's gone.
s11-phase-2-title: Natural language is the best API client.
s11-phase-2-desc: I don't need to remember Command lines flags or API endpoints like we did or do when we use the command prompts. I say what I want, and the agent figures out the how. Even the LLMs make mistakes !!! When something fails (wrong project, missing member), it tells me why and I fix it.
s11-phase-3-title: Quality scales when effort drops to zero.
s11-phase-3-desc: When adding a description costs nothing, every task gets one. When setting dependencies is free, every chain gets linked. The quality of project setup went up dramatically — not because I got better at it, but because the cost of doing it right became negligible.
s12-body: We help companies set up AI pipelines that create life-changing experiences — from whiteboard vision to structured project delivery, fully integrated into your existing tools.
s12-col-1-item: No internet, no cloud, no external APIs. Your data stays on your infrastructure. Period.
s12-col-2-item: Leveraging cloud services: Remote drives, Mistral, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, Llama, and public APIs.
s12-body-2: Choose your posture. We build both.

s13-bio:
Abdelkrim Boujraf is the founder of ALT-F1 SRL and Partner at XFlowData, based in Brussels & Tunisia.

Abdelkrim supports your industry with SOFTWARE, DATA, ANALYTICS & LEAN OPTIMISATIONS. He also yells at his AI agent in Discord.

Cloud, On-Premise, Digital Transformation, Digital Workplace, AI & Apps

If you manage projects and you're tired of the gap between "knowing what to do" and "actually configuring it in the tool" — this is worth trying.
